instruction: Rewrite the content to meet insurance regulatory and compliance standards.

input.draft_text:
What Is Cash Surrender Value?
Many life insurance policies have cash surrender value accruing over time. Upon cancellation or surrender of the policy, the insurance company is obligated to pay a portion or all of the cash surrender value accumulated on the policy. Here’s all you need to know about cash surrender value.
What is a cash surrender value?
Life insurance plans in India are broadly classified into three categories, namely term insurance plans, endowment plans and unit-linked insurance plans. Surrender benefit is available in two out of these three products, but cash surrender value can be determined only in one type.
Term Plans:Term life policies are the purest form of insurance. They last for a predetermined number of years, usually ranging between 10 – 35 years. They pay only death benefits if the policyholder (insured) dies within the policy term. There is no maturity or surrender benefit available with this plan. So, term life plans do not have any cash surrender value attached to them.
Endowment plans:
Endowment plans are savings-oriented life insurance plans which could last the entire life and build cash surrender value whilst active. It works as a savings + insurance vehicle. So, there is a guaranteed surrender benefit available for every endowment plan, which is the total amount receivable by the policyholder if he doesn't wish to continue the plan and wishes to withdraw the entire amount. It is important to note that cash surrender value is different from the sum assured, which refers to the coverage amount or death benefit of the policy. Cash surrender value is a pseudo cash account which is internal to the policy and is the means to offset the escalating cost of the coverage as the policyholders' age and corresponding risk factors increase. The cash surrender value in the endowment life insurance plan refers to the cash value net of the applicable surrender charges. The loan is available against most endowment plans upto 80-90% of the cash surrender value of the plan so that if the policyholder cannot repay the loan, the insurer can choose to surrender the plan and recover the loan amount.
Unit Linked Insurance plans:ULIPs or unit-linked insurance plans also can be surrendered, but there is no guaranteed surrender value for the same as the returns are linked to market performance. Hence ULIPs do not offer loans as there is no cash surrender value which is guaranteed at the beginning of the policy.
How can I access my cash surrender value?
If you have a cash crunch or financial distress, you can consider cancelling the policy, provided your insurance policy has generated substantial surrender value. By cancelling your policy, you will have access to the cash value accumulated against the policy. However, the such surrender value will be subject to surrender penalties and taxes. Upon cancellation of the policy, it is imperative to note that the coverage ceases to exist.
An alternative to cancelling a policy is a partial withdrawal of your cash surrender value. However, this option is available only in Unit Linked Insurance Plans and not in Endowment Plans. This option comes in handy when you are in financial distress or cannot afford premium payments. This alternative ensures that the coverage continues to remain in place. In Endowment plans, you can take a loan against your Cash Surrender Value if the cash requirement is temporary and you do not wish to surrender the plan altogether. Also, if you cannot pay subsequent premiums but you wish to continue the plan, you can convert the plan to a “paid up” policy, wherein you do not need to pay any further premium, but the amount invested with the insurer remains, and your policy continues with proportionately reduced benefits.
How to calculate surrender value?
The surrender cash value of your policy depends on the proportion of cash value against your life insurance policy and the surrender penalty applicable once you want to cancel it. The time period up to when the surrender period lasts and the way surrender charges are calculated are listed in your policy. They are a function of your age, gender, rating class, and the quantum of coverage against the policy.
The penalty is expressed as a percentage of the cash surrender value and typically decreases every year. There is a point at which the policy becomes “out of surrender, and thus, the penalty becomes zero. Endowment plans offer the guaranteed surrender benefit only if the premiums have been paid for a minimum of 3 years. During the initial years of the policy, the penalty can be quite steep. Surrender value typically builds up after the 3rd year. By the 5th year, the surrender value could be 80% of the built-up cash value. To calculate surrender value, you need to understand the nuances of the life insurance policy which you propose to surrender. The guaranteed surrender value of an endowment plan is usually 30% of the basic premium paid to date, excluding the first year’s premium, other than the additional premium paid for riders, loading, etc.
Suppose you intend to cancel your policy during the surrender period. In that case, you can reach out to your insurer, which will let you know the applicable cash surrender value and the surrender penalty.
Tax implications on surrender value:
The surrender or maturity benefit is completely tax-free under section 10(10D) of the Income Tax Act provided:
The sum assured is a minimum of 10 times1 the annualised premium for the plan in all years. If the policy had been taken before 1st April 20122, then this ratio is 5 times.
The amount is invested for a minimum period of 5 years. If the policy is surrendered before 5 years3, then the entire amount becomes taxable and gets added to your taxable income and is taxed as per slab. However, if it is surrendered after 5 years, then you can get tax benefits under section 10(10D).
For Unit Linked Insurance Plans, if the annual premium is less than Rs 2.5 lakhs per year. If the premium is more than Rs 2.5 lakhs, then the maturity benefit is taxed as per debt-equity tax norms prevailing at the time as per the norms of Budget 20213.
It is interesting to note that these taxes are applicable on the cash value, which net off the surrender penalty.
How to apply for cash surrender value?
You may want to cancel or surrender your policy for a variety of reasons. It is important to note that upon cancellation, the coverage ceases. As a policyholder, you need to fill out the surrender request form and submit it to the insurance company. Upon submission of the form, the insurer will process it and arrange for the release of funds via check or direct transfer.
There are other alternatives to surrendering the policy. Considering the cost levied upon cash surrender value in terms of penalty and taxes, it is suggested that you consider the alternatives before deciding to surrender the policy.
Alternatives to surrendering the policy:
It is wise to continue your life coverage, especially so if you need the coverage. You should check the below alternatives before you consider the cancellation of the policy.
Paid-up option:If you have not been able to pay your premiums, you can always choose to convert the plan into a “Paid Up” option and continue the plan4. Under this option, no further premium needs to be paid and the policy coverage gets proportionately reduced as per the reduced paid-up option life coverage, but the life coverage continues. Proportionately reduced maturity benefit is also paid out at the end of the policy tenure.
Partial withdrawal: Instead of cashing out your entire cash surrender value, you can choose to make a partial withdrawal for Unit Linked Insurance Plans. The amount can be withdrawn from the existing fund value as per the norms of the policy. If the partial withdrawal is made after 5 years5, then the amount is considered tax-free under section 10(10D) in the hands of the policyholder.
Loan against policy: If the policy has acquired a guaranteed surrender value, in the case of Endowment Plans, then there is a loan option of 80-90%6 of the surrender value, as specified in the policy document.
Remember to talk to your insurer and explore all other options to relieve you of your financial distress before you consider the cancellation of your policy. Surrendering your policy will cancel your life coverage. Hence this should be your last resort.
Ref:
https://www.incometaxindia.gov.in/news/circular-2-2022.pdf
https://economictimes.indiatimes.com/what-you-must-know-about-taxability-of-life-insurance-policy-payouts/tomorrowmakersshow/69425598.cms#:~:text=As%20per%20Section%2010(10D,receiver%20subject%20to%20certain%20conditions.
https://www.businesstoday.in/latest/story/is-the-surrender-value-of-ulip-taxable-310458-2021-10-26
https://economictimes.indiatimes.com/wealth/insure/what-is-a-paid-up-insurance-policy/articleshow/61995704.cms
https://outlookmoney.com/all-your-money-queries-answered/is-the-partial-withdrawal-from-the-ulip-corpus-taxable-1982
https://www.valueresearchonline.com/stories/51033/what-happens-on-surrendering-an-lic-policy-against-which-you-have-availed-a-loan
